raw_case:
Isolated eosinophilic mastitis mimicking carcinoma: A rare case report from Syria


Introduction and importance

Eosinophilic mastitis is a very rare but benign entity of complex pathogenesis. Most cases present similar to breast carcinoma, which makes it a challenging condition to diagnose. We report the sixteenth case of eosinophilic mastitis and the third case without systemic demonstrations. In this paper, we present the first case of eosinophilic mastitis from Syria and the third isolated one in the literature.

Case presentation

This case report discusses a 38-year-old female patient presented to the clinic of thoracic surgery at the Hospital on January 2020 with a pain and swelling in the left breast, with no remarkable personal or family history.

Clinical discussion

Eosinophilic mastitis is an extremely rare benign pathology that may be mistaken for breast carcinoma. However, eosinophilic mastitis can be diagnosed using a combination of ultrasound imaging and fine needle aspiration or ultrasound guided biopsy or excisional biopsy. Histopathology examination is the key tool to a proper diagnosis.

Conclusion

Eosinophilic mastitis is an extremely rare benign pathology that affects the breast tissue presenting in the form of an ill-defined mass and may be mistaken for breast carcinoma.

Highlights

•Eosinophilic mastitis is an extremely rare benign pathology that affects the breast tissue.•We report the sixteenth case of eosinophilic mastitis and the third case without systemic demonstrations.•Histopathology examination is the key tool to a proper diagnosis.1

Introduction

Eosinophilic mastitis is a very rare but benign entity of complex pathogenesis. It occurs when the breast is infiltrated with eosinophils causing a lump in the breast. Most cases present similar a breast carcinoma [1]. Only few cases were reported in the literature so far and the majority of them were accompanied with other manifestations such as Hypereosinophilic syndrome, peripheral eosinophilia, Churg-Strauss syndrome, urticaria and asthma [2]. However only two cases were reported to be isolated from any other association or involvement of other organ [2,3]. We present the first case of eosinophilic mastitis from Syria. To our knowledge, this is the sixteenth case published in the medical literature, and the third isolated case to be reported in the literature.This case report has been reported in line with SCARE Criteria [4].2

Case presentation

A 38-years old nonsmoker female, with no remarkable personal or family history, presented to the clinic of thoracic surgery at the Hospital on January 2020 with a pain and swelling in the left breast that started two weeks earlier and was associated with fever. The patient reports a random treatment with antibiotics for five days without any response. Physical examination revealed painful, tender and mobile mass in the lateral upper quadrant of the left breast with no discharges from the nipple. The right breast was normal on palpation, so was both axillae. The patient is a mother of three children, all of whom were breastfed, and stopped having children two years ago. There was no history of drug-intake especially contraceptive drug. There was no history of both appetite and weight loss, non-pulmonary, dermatology or rheumatology findings. Liver and kidney functions were normal. Ultrasound showed a thickness of both adeno and adipose tissues in the lateral half of the left breast with hypoechoic spots. There were no calcifications or enlarged lymph nodes and the distance behind the areola and around skin was normal. Blood tests were in normal ranges. White blood cells count was 8200/mm3, eosinophils count 85/mm3 (1 %). Differential Diagnosis was mostly suggestive of breast carcinoma. However, ultrasonography and computed tomography were inconclusive. Therefore, surgery was performed to remove the lesion.Macroscopically, the specimen shows an irregular mass of fibrous-fatty tissue measuring (9×7×6) cm in maximum dimension (Fig. 1), the cut section shows multi-lobulated nodule of firm gray-white fibro-fatty tissue.Microscopically (Fig. 2), the sections reveal proliferation of fibrous and fatty tissue surrounding the ducts and lobules. Some are dilated and other distorted with accumulation of fatty detritus in their lumen with diffuse infiltration by a large number of eosinophils and few foreign-body Giant cells. No cellular atypia noted.Diagnosis of eosinophilic mastitis was established and patient did not receive any further treatment. No evidence of parasitic infestation, bacterial or fungal, and tuberculosis culture was negative. Further investigations did not reveal any other complications or manifestationns. One-year and two- years follow-ups were normal.3

Discussion

Eosinophilic mastitis is considered one of the rarest cases in the medical literature. The importance of documenting these cases is that they may be confused with the diagnosis of breast cancer, which means completely different treatment options, outcomes, prognosis and results [1,2,12]. The few reports of eosinophilic mastitis mention that it is either associated with a condition causing blood eosinophilia, such as asthma or urticaria [5], or Churg Strauss syndrome [6] or hypereosinophilic syndrome [12]. Or it could present in the form of an isolated entity, as in two documented cases [1,3]. The infection can be unilateral or bilateral [6] and it can also occur in a breastfeeding woman [7]. The age of the affected women ranged between 15 and 60 years [8] and a male case of eosinophilia was reported [9].Although eosinophils may infiltrate into any organ, causing disturbance in its function, such as the lungs, heart, esophagus, stomach and intestines, their infiltration into the breast is rare [4]. Eosinophils are white blood cells that play an important role in innate immunity and countering parasitic, viral and septic infections and their act is similar when they migrate and colonize an organ, causing its immunological effects [7,10]. This would explain the proliferation of fibrous and fatty tissue surrounding the ducts and lobules and causing distortion to their architecture. The eosinophilic infiltration may be accompanied by histiocytes and/or plasma cells.Isolated eosinophilic mastitis that is not associated with any systemic involvement or peripheral blood eosinophilia is very rare as only two cases were reported making ours the third [1,3]. The etiologic process in our case is not understood as there is no systemic demonstrations, neither peripheral eosinophilia, infections of any nature, drugs, allergies or malignancies.The lesions mostly have asymmetric morphology and poorly defined borders. The consistency of the lesions varies between tender and hard, mobile or firm or even associated with peculiar skin appearance that of a peau d'orange. Ultrasound imaging would show hyperechoic breast tissue and/or distortion of the structure of lobules and ducts which is unspecific and could also indicate malignancy. As Parakh et al. reported, imaging studies were mostly inconclusive in ruling out malignancy in the cases reported so mostly fine needle aspiration or ultrasound guided biopsy or excisional biopsy are the main diagnostic method [3]. Biopsy was the critical criterion in the diagnosis of our case and histopathologic examination confirmed eosinophilic mastitis ruling out malignancy.In terms of treatment, due to the benign course of eosinophilic, it is typically treated with medication such as prednisolone and it may or may not be accompanied by antihistamines depending on the underlying etiology. Surgical excision is also a treatment option although is an aggressive one [1,10]. However, our case, did not need any further treatment other than surgical excision due to the isolated nature of the lesion and the clean borders of the excision. Follow-up plays an important role, as the medical literature reported a case of recurrence after 2 years [11]. However, our patient is recurrence-free for two years now.4

Conclusion

Eosinophilic mastitis is an extremely rare benign pathology that affects the breast tissue presenting in the form of an ill-defined mass and may be mistaken for breast carcinoma, therefore, it can be difficult to identify. However, eosinophilic mastitis can be diagnosed and treated efficiently using a combination of ultrasound imaging and fine needle aspiration or ultrasound guided biopsy or excisional biopsy. Histopathology examination is the key tool to a proper diagnosis. In our case, the result of the surgery was good and our patient did not need any subsequent procedure or drug treatment.

Provenance and peer review

Not commissioned, externally peer-reviewed.

Ethical approval

No ethical approval was needed for this case report.

Consent for publication

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for the publication of this case report and accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal.

Research registration number

Not applicable.

Sources of funding

This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 38-year-old female, nonsmoker.
- **Chief Complaint:** Pain and swelling in the left breast.
- **History of Present Illness:** Onset of symptoms two weeks prior, associated with fever, and unresponsive to a five-day antibiotic treatment.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** No history of drug use, contraceptive use, or systemic symptoms.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Tender, mobile mass in the lateral upper quadrant of the left breast; no nipple discharge, no abnormalities in the right breast or axillae.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Bloodwork: Normal ranges, including eosinophil count (1%).
  - Ultrasound: Thickened adenose and adipose tissues with hypoechoic spots; no calcifications or enlarged lymph nodes.
  - Imaging: Ultrasonography and CT inconclusive for malignancy.
  - Histopathology: Proliferation of fibrous and fatty tissue with eosinophilic infiltration, distorted ducts and lobules, no cellular atypia or malignancy. Negative findings for parasitic, bacterial, fungal, and tuberculosis infections.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Consideration of Breast Carcinoma:**
   - Rationale: Ill-defined breast mass, tenderness, and imaging findings are suspicious for carcinoma.
   - Excluded: Lack of malignancy markers in histopathology, including no cellular atypia and confirmed benign eosinophilic infiltration.
   
2. **Infectious or Parasitic Cause:**
   - Rationale: Fever and local inflammation could suggest infection or parasitic etiology.
   - Excluded: Negative cultures for bacterial, fungal, and tuberculosis; no evidence of parasitic infestation.

3. **Systemic Eosinophilic Disorders:**
   - Rationale: Eosinophilic mastitis is often associated with hypereosinophilic syndrome or other eosinophil-related systemic conditions.
   - Excluded: Normal eosinophil count and absence of systemic manifestations or blood eosinophilia.

4. **Benign Eosinophilic Mastitis:**
   - Rationale: Histopathology revealed characteristic eosinophilic infiltration and architectural distortion typical of eosinophilic mastitis. Imaging confirmed the absence of malignancy or systemic eosinophilic involvement.
   - Confirmed: The diagnosis satisfies criteria for isolated eosinophilic mastitis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Isolated eosinophilic mastitis of the left breast, a rare benign condition. The diagnosis was supported by histopathological findings of eosinophilic infiltration and exclusion of systemic or infectious causes. Surgical excision was definitive, with no further treatment needed and no recurrence at two-year follow-up.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Initial assessment of the patient's presentation: A 38-year-old female presents with localized left breast pain and swelling of two weeks duration, accompanied by fever. Initial antibiotic treatment was ineffective, suggesting a non-bacterial or antibiotic-resistant infection, or a non-infectious inflammatory process.

<step 2> Review of physical examination findings: A tender, mobile mass in the left breast's upper lateral quadrant is noted. The absence of nipple discharge and normal findings in the right breast and axillae are important to narrow down the differential diagnosis, making widespread or systemic conditions less likely initially.

<step 3> Analysis of ancillary tests, starting with bloodwork: Normal bloodwork, including a normal eosinophil count (1%), is somewhat helpful in excluding systemic eosinophilic conditions at first glance, but importantly, it does not rule out localized tissue eosinophilia.

<step 4> Interpretation of imaging results: Ultrasound reveals thickened adenose and adipose tissues with hypoechoic spots but no calcifications or enlarged lymph nodes. Both Ultrasound and CT were inconclusive for malignancy, reducing the likelihood of cancer as the primary diagnosis, but not entirely excluding it without histopathology.

<step 5> Critical evaluation of histopathology: This is the most crucial piece of evidence. The report describes a proliferation of fibrous and fatty tissue with significant eosinophilic infiltration, distorted ducts and lobules, but no cellular atypia or malignancy.  Notably, tests for parasitic, bacterial, fungal, and tuberculosis infections are negative. This histopathology profile is highly suggestive of a specific inflammatory condition characterized by eosinophilic infiltration in the breast tissue.

<step 6> Synthesizing all findings to formulate a differential diagnosis: Considering the patient's age, clinical presentation (pain, swelling, mass, fever), antibiotic resistance, and the key histopathological finding of eosinophilic infiltration without evidence of infection or malignancy, the primary differential diagnoses should include inflammatory breast conditions with eosinophilia. Conditions like bacterial mastitis are less likely due to antibiotic failure and negative bacterial tests.  Malignancy is less likely given the inconclusive imaging and negative histopathology for atypia.

<step 7> Focusing on eosinophilic mastitis: The histopathology report is strongly indicative of **Eosinophilic Mastitis**. This is a rare, benign inflammatory breast condition characterized by eosinophilic infiltration of the breast tissue.  It aligns perfectly with the findings of fibrous and fatty tissue proliferation, eosinophilic infiltration, distorted ducts and lobules, and absence of infection or malignancy.  While the peripheral eosinophil count is normal, localized tissue eosinophilia is the defining feature of this condition.

<step 8> Excluding other less likely diagnoses: Other inflammatory conditions like granulomatous mastitis or periductal mastitis might be considered, but the prominent eosinophilic infiltration makes eosinophilic mastitis the most probable diagnosis, especially given the absence of granulomas or other specific features in the histopathology that would point towards these alternatives.

<step 9> Final Diagnostic Conclusion: Based on the comprehensive analysis of the patient's clinical presentation, ancillary tests, and most importantly, the histopathology report demonstrating eosinophilic infiltration and excluding other etiologies, the most likely diagnosis is Eosinophilic Mastitis.

### Answer:
Eosinophilic Mastitis
